title: Asia-Pacific Carriers Lean Into SAF as CORSIA's Hard Phase Begins
dek: As CORSIA's first mandatory offsetting phase tightens in 2026, Asian and Gulf carriers including Singapore Airlines, ANA, JAL and Emirates are scaling sustainable aviation fuel uptake despite stubbornly thin regional supply.
body:
Sustainable aviation fuel has moved from pilot projects to procurement strategy across the Asia-Pacific and the Gulf, even as it remains a fraction of the kerosene burned each day. With the International Civil Aviation Organization's CORSIA scheme entering its first mandatory phase in 2026, carriers from Singapore Airlines and ANA to Emirates and Cathay Pacific are weighing how blended SAF, efficiency gains and offsets fit together on the long road to the industry's 2050 net-zero target.

Why SAF still moves slowly in Asia

SAF is a drop-in fuel, chemically similar enough to conventional jet fuel that it can be blended and used in today's engines without modification. The barrier is not the aircraft but the supply chain: most volume today is made from waste fats and oils through the HEFA pathway, feedstocks that are limited and contested. Production capacity has expanded, including Neste's large facility in Singapore, yet output across the region is still small relative to demand, and prices typically run well above fossil kerosene.

For Asia-Pacific carriers, that creates a familiar tension. Several have signed offtake agreements or run trial uplifts at hubs such as Changi, Haneda and Hong Kong, but the economics depend heavily on policy support, feedstock access and the pace at which newer pathways scale.

How CORSIA and national mandates intersect

CORSIA asks airlines to offset growth in international emissions above a baseline, with the period from 2024 widely treated as the first phase in which obligations bite for participating states. SAF use can reduce an operator's offsetting requirement, giving carriers a financial reason to blend fuel alongside buying eligible credits. The detail matters, and many airlines describe their planning in cautious terms while rules and credit eligibility settle.

  • Singapore has signalled a SAF target for departing flights with a levy-based funding model.
  • Japanese carriers ANA and JAL have set blending goals toward 2030 and beyond.
  • Gulf operators including Emirates and Etihad continue demonstration flights and offtake deals.

Beyond fuel, airlines lean on measurable efficiency levers: fleet renewal with types such as the A350 and 787, lighter cabins, and optimised flight paths where airspace allows.

The near-term reality is incremental. SAF will likely supply only a slim share of Asia-Pacific uplift in 2026, but the combination of CORSIA pressure, emerging national mandates and long-dated offtake contracts is steadily shifting it from symbolism toward a structural, if still costly, part of the regional fuel mix.
heroImageAlt: A widebody jet refuelling at an Asian airport gate with a fuel bowser connected beneath the wing
metaTitle: Asia-Pacific Carriers Scale SAF as CORSIA Phase Tightens
metaDescription: Singapore Airlines, ANA, JAL and Emirates expand sustainable aviation fuel use as CORSIA's mandatory phase tightens in 2026, though regional SAF supply stays thin.
